speaker: interviewer
text: --95, Tape two.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, as a young girl growing up in Hungary, I was always hoping for a better future. But what happened to our future? For our dreams? On March 19, in 1944, when the German SS men came into Hungary-- and our Passover was that time early in March-- I remember it was finished on a Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who was at your table with that-- that Passover? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother came back from the labor camp just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: --two-- Jeno--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeno.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just two weeks before they-- we were taken away. So he was there. Grandfather was there, from-- my mother's father; my mother, my sister, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frieda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frieda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frieda. And I'm just thinking, I think my younger brother wasn't home that time. Of course, my other brother, Adolf, was away in labor camp. So we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any cousins--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not that many.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were there living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The cousins, they had their own family seder. And news were circling around that they were taking Jewish people away from the other cities and other town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn about that from that Polish Jew who escaped, or was it more--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, among-- and before that, already, in-- in Hungary, in 1944, when we went out, we had to wear the Jewish star on our clothes and our yellow band on our coat, or dress, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that come?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month or the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. No. No. Actually, in 19-- end of 1943, already you could feel it wasn't-- something wasn't right. And then if you wanted to, you couldn't-- we had curfew-- that Jewish people couldn't go out and leave the house after 5 o'clock. If you had to go for any reason to the next town or something, you had to have a paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happens if you just went out and you were caught? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, because we didn't try that. We wouldn't dare, you know, fool around with those kind of a things. And so it was very, very hard, the living. Actually, I was kinda afraid-- very afraid-- to even go out from one room to the kitchen to get a glass of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or I remember one time, these were the Hungarian soldiers came to our village, and they were looking for places where to stay. So grandfather had an extra room. And they took the room, and they made themselves comfortable. They were sleeping there. It-- it-- I mean, it was just not right. You know what I'm trying to tell you? Mm-hmm, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- it was something coming on what we couldn't understand, what really was gonna happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think of going to another country or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- we really didn't think of anything, because we didn't even-- I don't even think you could go any place anymore. Because I had relatives that were leaving the country in 1940, and I think that was the last chance you could get out. So after that, you couldn't even go anyplace after 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after Passover was finished, Saturday evening, I remember it was a knock on the door. And-- and they were coming to tell us that-- Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the 'they'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some neighbor or some friends, that they were taking people away from next town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Tarcal. We had a lot of relatives over there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother was there-- your younger--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the younger one. But after-- before all this, they escaped. They went up to Budapest. They ran away from there, thanks God-- I mean that time. And then we got the news, Sunday morning. Again, that guy came with the drum, and everybody was gathering outside the street. And he was giving notice that we have to get ready, because we have to leave our home. And we could only take so many things what we could carry in our hand. And we had to congregate in our synagogue yard. And I remember we didn't had no bread in the house. And the first thing my mother and my sister was doing, to start bake bread. At least, we should have bread. And we left the Passover dishes just the way it was. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Followed the instruction, whatever what we had to do. There was nothing. We had everything there. The livestock was there, and-- and everything was there. And Sunday, by the evening, we were at the synagogue yard. And it was very, very painful to leave the house, just-- [CRYING] --just walk out and leave everything. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] We took the bag in our hand, and next day, Monday-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOB] [CRYING] --we had to walk to the railroad station and schlep whatever we could. But before that, my mother made to put on a few dresses so it was lighter to carry the things, and under her-- and the same with my sister. And-- and this was the Jewish-- I mean, the Hungarian policemen. You know, they made us walk fast, and then they make us sit down. And people who couldn't stand up quickly and move on, they would hit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother wasn't able to get up very quickly. And I remember he-- she got hit, you know? And that was terrible to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we went on the train to Satoraljaujhel That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was S-A-T-O-R-A-L-J-A-U-J-H-E-L. And that was a ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it north? South? West?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was Zemplen. It was Zemplen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same province, like.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same providence, yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Zemplen, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zemplen, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Z-E-M-P-L-E-N. And over there they bought in Jewish people from all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Satoraljaujhel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Satoraljaujhel?

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Shatee'

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- it wasn't-- it wasn't that far-- maybe-- maybe an hour or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a regular train you were on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a regular train we went. And it was filled up only with Jewish people. And just going back-- I forgot to tell you this. When they-- that was the Sunday when we had to get ready. They already were taking, from Tarcal, the Jewish people through our town, to the train station. And the older people who couldn't walk, they put them on a horse wagon. And the horse were taking them. And the rest of them, they were walking. And I remember I was standing out there with a pail of water, and asked them if they wanted to have some water to drink-- at least that much. And I saw my relatives sitting in the wagon. They were just waving. We couldn't even talk to them. They were my grandmother's brother and sister who used to live in Tarcal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got into the ghetto, over there it was-- So let's go back to you were on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the train brought you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Satoraljaujhel.

speaker: interviewer
text: A station that was in 'Shatoroy'.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that was the ghetto what they closed up a certain portion of the city. And they jammed there-- I don't know how many thousands of-- of Jews over there. And they put us into the house. And then it was a room. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: They escorted you to a house, or told you where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just-- any house. They were taking other people's houses, and they just put the people in there. And that was no question asked if they wanted it or not. That's-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They just took you there, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took us there. And I remember this was a very big room. And it was a lot of people there. And grandfather didn't come with us. They took him to the Jewish hospital in the ghetto. And we stayed there about maybe three weeks or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were-- lived in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people. I really didn't count at that, but it was young children and-- and older--

speaker: interviewer
text: Strangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange people-- strange people And they had a kitchen set up where they were cooking, and that's where we had to go for our meal. And they just were giving so much to eat. We couldn't leave the ghetto at all. We were watched. We were guarded all the time. And we could go and visit grandfather, because that was in the same section. And after about maybe three weeks or so, [SIGHS] then, again-- that was in early May-- when they told us--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you were there that three or four weeks? What did you do every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing too much. You were just going out to the street, talking to the people. There was nothing too much there to do at all-- nothing. And thinking back, we really-- we didn't realize what was in store for us, what's happening around us. And in early May, they were starting to empty the ghetto. And actually, we were the very first transport who were put on the cattle car. And we were jammed in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe, like, how it happened. Who came to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who came to the room you were staying in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I don't. I can't remember, because it was so confusion over there already by that time when they told us that they gonna take us to work someplace, a different place. We gonna go. And, of course, we believed them. And it wasn't so. And we had to go to the train station again. And it wasn't a regular train anymore. And they jammed in a lot of people in one of those cattle cars. And they were-- there was a pail of water and an empty pail for sanitary reason. And after that, they locked the door. And it was a horrible feeling, you know, when you're locked up. And you know-- there was no window-- and you know you can't go no place. And if we could find a little place on the floor to sit down, we did. My father wanted it very badly for my older brother and sister to escape someplace, but there was no place to go. And-- and-- and-- and they wouldn't go anyway. They wanted to be with the family together. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all together in this train? Your father and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, yes, and my aunt, my mother's sister, with the five children. One of them was my age. And then another boy was about two years younger; and three little girls. And well, what can I tell you? It was just awful with those little kids to-- to go through all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father was there and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, my older brother, Jeno, and my sister Frieda-- oh, and my aunt, who was my brother's sister. She escaped to us with her son from Czechoslovakia, because they took her husband away. And she thought if she comes to us, she'd be in a safer place. But we came in together with--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: --her. Her-- her name was Rozsika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rozsika.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I can't recall my cousin's-- cousin name.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember he was a tall, young man. And [SIGHS] so we were riding in this cattle train-- it seems like forever. It stopped once to empty the pail, and perhaps give some more water. And I don't know how long it's supposed to last to go from-- from Hungary to Poland, where we find out later on, you know, where we wind up. But the train stopped for a while. It was just sitting there, and we didn't know why. And then later on, we find out why we had to wait. We arrived during the night in Auschwitz-Birkenau. We find out later. First we didn't know what it was or what kind of place was it. But that-- during the night, they wouldn't open up the door. And we-- we could hear crying and screaming. And we could smell a funny smell. And my father was able to look out that little window, what they have on the cattle train. And he saw flames shooting up from the chimney. And he made a remark in-- in Hebrew-- in Jewish, what my sister related later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the remark with?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he said we came to hell. This is hell. When he saw the flames shooting up, I don't know what made him say that. But I guess, I don't know. He-- he had a feeling he wouldn't be able to survive it or something. Well, anyway, in the morning, [SIGHS] early, they opened the door. And then everything broke loose. Then we saw the SS mens and the-- the German shepherd dogs. And we saw prisoners. We didn't know, actually, who they were, but they were wearing that gray stripes-- the men. They had to work with the transport who were coming in. And everything had to be very, very quickly. We had to get off from the-- jump off from the cattle cars. And schnell, schnell, the SS men said. And the women had to be on one side, the men on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was separated so quickly from my mother that-- that afterwards, I was looking for her. And I was trying to run back to her, actually, twice. And the SS man saw me, and he pushed me back the first time. And, again, the second time-- again he pushed me back. And he-- he was kinda angry, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, you-- you schwein Jew-- you dirty Jew. He said, you're young. You can work. I don't know. I really should have thanked him, because otherwise I wouldn't be sitting here and tell you my story. And-- but I didn't understand. I didn't know what we came into. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak German? Did you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand German. I don't speak German, but my grandmother always talked to me Jewish. And, you know, the Jewish language is very like German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I could understand what he meant, and I could see his anger, too. And, of course, I was right next to my sister, Frieda, and my aunt Rose, who was my father's sister. And we were just standing there and waiting, and amazed, just looking around to see what kind of a place is this. I mean, we saw so many people. It just-- it was just unbelievable. And after that, as we were standing there, we were waiting that they should take us in. They took everything away from us that time. Whatever little thing, what we had in our hand, we had to just throw everything away in one pile there. And as we were waiting for our turn to go into the sauna, over there to-- we didn't know what they were going to do. We had to get rid of all those dress, what I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They called it a sauna? They said you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it a sauna.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sauna. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually de-lice-- they disinfected us. They cut our hair, they painted something, you know, under the arm and under, you know, our female section. And they give us any kinda clothes. If it fit, it was good. If it didn't, it was-- it was OK. Like, my sister's were tight on her. Mine was loose. Some girls get a party dress. I mean, it just-- you know, we looked like-- I don't know. It's just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I was standing right to my sister, and I was hollering for her, Frieda, Frieda. And she was hollering for me, because we just didn't recognize each other. And after that, I-- I just got a hold of her. I shouldn't lose her, because I wouldn't-- Did they just cut your hair short when you said they cut your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not short-- bald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bald.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just bald. And they promised that we gonna be reunited with our family on Sunday. But that was just the very, very first lie what they told us. That Sunday never came. And-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- after you found your sister, Frieda--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were-- you said you were yelling for one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened? After you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --were clutching her.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she was-- she was hiding. I always-- I had to-- right away, we had to stand up five in a row, like soldiers. And she was the very first one always in the-- in the line. And I had to be behind her all the time. So this way-- and my aunt was-- actually, five of us made a row, because my brother's fiancee, and her aunt, and my aunt. So we made five in a row. And that's the was we were always standing there when we were counted for appells, like when Mengele came and looking us over every single day to see-- he was-- who was, you know, sickly and-- and who could go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? Tell me about Mengele coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, when we were selected to be sent to the right side, that-- that was a good sign, because, you know, we know we were gonna go to work. The one who, like my mother, and her sister, and the children, were put to the left. And they didn't have a chance anymore. And they told us that we gonna be tattooed. And my number was A6145. And my sister's was A6144, because she was in front of me. And that meant life for us. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you tattooed right after they shaved your hair and every--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No-- about a couple days after that we were tattooed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after they shaved your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in-- we-- first of all, we had to stay in line already in appell. They called it appell when the soldiers came and they counted how many people were there. And then they put us in a wooden barrack with a-- a wood hut. And it had beds. Only the beds were there, and three on each row. And so that's where we were sleeping. But-- and this was in Auschwitz-Birkenau. That was the most terrible place you could have been.
